what if we add a command that locks until the agent receives a message -- we could instruct it on the session-start hook to run it in the background so that whenever it receives a mention the command completes and the agent acctually sees it (when the command completes we should remind it to re-run it again to wait in the background for the next incoming message)

Surfaced a design fork: a blocking `wait-for-mention` command is genuinely useful for active/autonomous agents, but for idle agents between prompts it may not add reactivity over the existing inbox injection. Asked which case is being optimized for.
I think you're wrong, if an agent uses a shell command running it in the background, when the command exits, an idle agent will be awoken, in all agent harnesses that I've tried that's the case

Accepted the correction and outlined the clean end-to-end design: the hook injects an instruction telling the agent to run `wait-for-mention` itself, the command polls the SQLite inbox, and on mention it prints and exits with a re-run reminder. Noted the hook must inject the instruction rather than spawn the process itself.
no, no `&` just say "in the background" like "run_in_background=true"
Confirmed the agent should run the command via Bash with `run_in_background: true`, and the re-run reminder should match that phrasing. Asked whether to implement.

Built the command: polls the SQLite inbox every 500ms, does a relay self-fetch on startup, prints pending mentions plus a re-run reminder on completion, and exits cleanly on a 5-minute default timeout.
are we injecting somewhere the "remember to run tenex-edge wait-for-mention" command during session start?
Confirmed the instruction prints once into the session's system context at startup, and the command's own output reminds the agent to re-run.
are we using that script in my cluade code config?
Confirmed the settings wire `tenex-edge-hook.py session-start` and it's live, injecting the instruction at the start of every new session.
poiny my config to this tree so we don't have the same problem again -- and delete the one in in ~/.local
Updated all three harnesses (Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode) to inject the `wait-for-mention` instruction and point directly to the source tree; deleted the deployed copy in `~/.local`.
review the claude code docs -- is something supposed to run? I literally don't see antything happening when I start a session

Explained that the hook is running (session shows up in the fabric) but SessionStart hook stdout is injected as silent system context, not displayed in the TUI, and the agent can't act on it until its first active turn. Suggested moving the instruction to `UserPromptSubmit` so it fires when the agent is actually active.
the harness is very obviously not running absolutely anything, there's clearly no llm call as soon as I start cluade code that would trigger the run of wait-for-mentions

Confirmed the deployed copy was deleted and settings point to the source tree. Moved the instruction to fire once per session on the first `UserPromptSubmit` turn, when the agent is active and can run the command.
Explained the instruction didn't appear because this wasn't the first turn of the session — the flag file was already written earlier. Advised starting a fresh session to see it fire.
